serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of steamed white rice as served in a Japanese restaurant, approximately 150 g cooked weight.

nutrient_source.title: Rice, white, short-grain, cooked (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 169760)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked short-grain white rice. Short-grain japonica rice is the standard variety used in Japanese cuisine. Values are per 100 g cooked weight.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice, short-grain, cooked
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is the published mean value for cooked short-grain white rice (japonica type) from the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Foster-Powell et al. (2002) Am J Clin Nutr 76:5–56. GL per serving calculated as (72 × 28.7 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 150 g serving) / 100 = 31.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (1 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Plain steamed white rice is very low in sodium (approximately 1.5 mg per 150 g serving) and does not contribute meaningfully to blood pressure elevation; however, hypertensive individuals should monitor sodium-rich accompaniments such as soy sauce, pickles, or miso soup served alongside.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 72) and high glycemic load per serving (~31)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed white rice has a high GI of 72 and a glycemic load of approximately 31 per standard 150 g bowl, which can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, consider substituting with lower-GI alternatives such as brown rice or mixed-grain rice, and pair with protein and non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat and negligible cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain steamed white rice is very low in saturated fat (0.05 g per 100 g) and contains negligible cholesterol, making it a neutral food for cardiovascular risk when consumed in moderate portions; frequent high-volume consumption of refined white rice has been associated epidemiologically with increased cardiometabolic risk, so portion control and dietary variety are advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed white rice (Japanese: ご飯, gohan) is the foundational staple of Japanese cuisine, prepared by washing and steaming short-grain japonica rice until tender and slightly sticky. The cooked grains are soft, moist, and mildly sweet, with a characteristic cohesive texture that makes them easy to eat with chopsticks. In Japanese culinary tradition, plain rice is served as the central carbohydrate component of a meal alongside protein dishes, miso soup, and pickled vegetables. Per 100 g cooked weight, it provides approximately 130 kcal, 28.7 g carbohydrate, 2.4 g protein, and 0.2 g fat, making it a high-carbohydrate, low-fat food. The glycemic index is high (GI 72), and a standard 150 g bowl carries a glycemic load of approximately 31, which is clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. Sodium content is negligible in plain form, making it suitable for hypertensive diets when consumed without high-sodium condiments. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal. In Singapore, steamed rice is served across Japanese restaurant chains such as Sukiya as a side or base for gyudon (beef bowl) and curry dishes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of Japanese rice?
Is white rice good for diabetics?
What is the GI of Japanese rice?
How much carbohydrate in a bowl of rice?
Is Japanese rice the same as short-grain rice?
meal_context: Accompaniment to teriyaki and grilled dishes
Side dish in Japanese bento boxes

GI value of 72 is well-established in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature for japonica short-grain white rice.
notes: trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; white rice contains no trans fats by nature. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source; cooked white rice contains negligible free sugars. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as rice contains no ascorbic acid. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — naturally zero in plain white rice. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based grain food. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero in plain rice. vitamin_e_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source for cooked short-grain white rice. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as rice is a plant food. The food_name in the input ('610. Rice') has been normalized to the canonical English name 'Steamed White Rice' to remove menu numbering and improve specificity. The cuisine context is Japanese (Sukiya restaurant chain). Nutrient values are for plain cooked rice without added salt, oil, or seasoning. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 169760.
